title: Evening star. [volume]

text:
District's Collections
From St. Elizabeth's
Patients Increase
* Collections by the District from
patients at St. Elizabeth’s Hospital
are expected to amount to $424,000
during the current fiscal year ending
June 30, $110,000 more than last
year. The collections may bring the
percentage of reimbursements in the
District above those in the States.
Mrs. Olive G. Faircloth, attorney
in charge of the special financial
unit of the Collector of Taxes of
fice, said the percentage of reim
bursements in the District—more
than 13 per cent—was ahead of the
9 per cent for Massachusetts, usually
regarded as having the best per
centage.
Increasing Yearly.
Payments so far have totaled
$391,322, according to figures from
the office. During the fiscal year of
1942, the collections amounted to
only $203,396, but have steadily risen
in subsequent years with the estab
lishment of the special financial unit,
officials said.
Mrs. Faircloth said she has sought
additional personnel for the unit,
which is now faced with the task of
collecting from the families of in
mates of the District Training School
at Laurel, Md., under new legisla
tion. The office also is charged with
me responsibility of collecting de
linquent accounts turned over by
Gallinger Municipal Hospital, Up
shur Street and Glenn Dale Sani
torium.
During the first nine months of
the present fiscal year, the unit has
obtained $13,404 from delinquent ac
counts of the municipal hospitals,
as well as hospitals with which the
District has accounts. Emergency,
Casualty and Children's Hospitals.
Veterans Transferred.
As a result of investigations by
the unit into St. Elizabeth cases, it
was said a number of veterans who
have been paid for by the District
have been transferred to the vet
erans rolls or removed to other Gov
ernment hospitals. One case is now
pending before the Justice Depart
ment to relieve the city from pay
ing for a man taken to the hospital
from District Jail, where he was held
on imigration charges.
In another case, it was said pay
ment was obtained back to 1905 for
a patient who died at the age of 105.
Buy War Bonds like our fighters
fight—all out for victory! And buy
them the way they buy them—the
easy way, the best way — through
payroll sayings.
Thieves Borrow Torch
To Open Stolen Safe
Thieves stole a safe from the
Steuart Motor Co., 300 H street
N.E., Sunday night and then cut
it open on the premises with
one of the firm’s acetylene torches,
police were told yesterday.
The manager. Raymond Carnell,
said the torch was taken from the
repair department. Only $67 in
cash was taken, he said.

OPENING OF HAVANA UNIVERSITY
AMERICAN GOVERNOR ATTEND9
AND IT ENTHUSIASTICALLY
APPLAUDED.
Havana, Oct
From un early hour every seat In
the big university auditorium was
filled and the doors and windows on
All sluuu were crowded. The arrival
of Governor Taft was the signal for
enthusiastic applause. He entered
accompanied by Assistant Secretary
State Dacon Captain McCoy, his
personal aide-de-camp the American
gilnister, Mr. Morgan, and Judge
Hchoenrlch of Porto Rico, and was
conducted by I)r. Derriel, rector of
the university, and' Lincoln de Zayas,
acting

t.—The
annual open­
ing of the University of Havana was
the occasion of a preat demonstration
of confidence in the provisional gov
ernment and of high appreciation of
Governor Taft and the work Presi
dent Roosevelt's commissioners have
undertaken. Those present included
the flower of Havana's most intellec
tual and refined society and their out
bursts of applause were a significant
indication of appreciation of the pro
visional government by the most
thoughtful element of the capital.
secretary of public instruction,
fcetween lines of university professors
in cap uud gown to the presiding iseat
In
the center of the platform. The
ipplause continued throughout the
time occupUd by tho governor's en
trance and left no doubt of the genu
bienosa of his welcome. After the
annual address on the history and
progress of the university, which was
delivered by Dr. Manuel Rodriguez,
professor of mythology, Governor
Taft presented the diplomas to the
successful students and then made a
notable address, which lasted half an
hour, the spirit of which was that the
Intervention of the United States was
undertaken solely to assist Cuba and
the Cubans and that her future was
dependent upon her young men.
As the governor and his party were
leaving the university the students
and others crowded about Mr. Taft's
automobile, cheering for President
Roosevelt, Governor Taft, the United
States and Cuba. Mr. Taft and his
party were then driven to the Amer
ican legation.
MORE MARINES LANDED.
Brigade Fully Equipped
for
Long Stay
Put Ashore.
Havana. Oct. 2.—A brigade of ma
rines consisting of 750 men, command
ed by Major Wendell G. Melville, was
landed at 8 a. m. from the United
States warships Kentucky, Indiana
Mtid Mlnneai oils and proceeded by trol
ley to Camp Columbia. There was no
tflpmonstrnticn of any kind.
All the mai^nes were landed within
two hours from the starting of the
-^operation, which wa9 carried out with
S'eat precision, owing to the fact that
**mn!tor and general eaulpmont
were'Tiroiight aloAg in tlie boats w»t..
each detachment, so that each boat
load of men had with them full equip
ment for a long stay ashore. Several
field guns were also landed.
All the landings wore made at the
new wharf of the Havana Central
electric railway. Thirty street cars
and several freight cara for the sui»
pliee were run directly out upon the
long pier and the ship's boats, laden
with marines, were towed to the
shore in strings of three and four by
the launches. As the marines, with
their equipment, reached the wharf
they were passed into the street cars,
which at once started, two or three at
a time, for Camp Columbia. The
freight cars followed as fast as they
were loaded. All the cars passed over
the Havana Central tracks to near the
arsenal, wlfere they were switched to
the City line, thence going through
the city to Camp Columbia. The sight
of so many carloads of troops created
considerable interest, but there was
not the least excitement.
FOR USE AS TRANSPORTS.
United Fruit Company Ordered to
Turn Over Vessels.
Philadelphia, Oct. 2.—The United
Fruit company, which employs a fleet
of steamships between the United
States and the West Indies, has re
ceived orders to turn over to the fed
eral government the steamers Ad
miral Farragut, Admiral Schley and
Admiral Sampson for use in trans
porting troops to Cuba. The Admiral
Farragut is in port here, the Admiral
Sampson is at Boston and the Ad
miral Schley is at New York. The
steamers are owned by the American
Mail Steamship company of New
York.
Will Remain at Pensacola.
Washington, Oct. 2.—The orders di
recting the dispatch of forty marines
from the Pensacola navyyard have
been revoked. A dispatch from Pen
sacola says the men are needed at
that place to preserve order and pro
tect property owing to conditions aris
ing from the recent hurricane.
TO PAY RESPECTS TO TAFT
Member* «f Supreme Court Call at
Legation.
Havana, Oct. 2.—The members of
the supreme court called at the lega
tion during the morning to pay their
respects to Governor Taft, who had
gone to the university to participate
in the annual opening.
Tho governor made a speech at the
opening of the university, which was
enthusiastically applauded by the
large audience, including the leading
people of the capital, who cheered
Mr. Taft, the United States and Pres
ident Roosevelt.
Major Eugene F. Ladd of the dis
armament commission passed the day
in the camps of Generals Pino Guerra
and Castillo, completing t&* disarm
ing arrangements.
No More Warships Needed.
Washington, Oct. 2.—The battleship
Texas has been held at Cape Henry,
awaiting further instructions from Sec
retary Taft. The secretary wired the
navy department that he did not need
any more battleships, but wanted more
marines He has been informed that
the Texas has 2on marines aboard and
further supplies for land service.
Yellow Fever at Havana.
Washington, Oct. 2.—The marine
hospital service ha9 been notified by
cable from Havana of the appearance
of two new cases of yellow fever there,
making seven cases reported during
tbe past week
OPPOSED TO CHINESE
GOVERNOR MAGOON DOES NOT
BELIEVE THEY WILL PROVE
A 8UCCESS ON CANAL.
Wtftt'ffl&Y WHEN HELD ft PEONAGE
ASSERTS UNITED STATES OUGHT
NOT TOLERATE ADOPTI&K
OF THAT SYSTEM.
mmmtm
New York, Oct. 2.—Charles E. Ma
goon, the retiring American minister
to Panama and governor of the Canal
zone, has arrived here on the steamer
Panama from Colon. Discussing con
ditions on the isthmus Governor Ma
goon said:
"The work on the isthmus Is going
on well. I can safely say that the
government of the #one is as good aa
that of any other locality in this coun
try. My relations with the Panama
government have been most pleasant
throughout.
"The canal work is progressing aB
rapidly as possible, but as yet it is
merely the work of preparation. The
main difficulty there is the labor ques
tion. Our labor Is drawn from Jamaica
and Parbados. Those people are in
telligcnt and with some education, but
the trouble Is that theiWest Indian is
a bird of passage. You'get him to un
derstand what you want and fairly
educated to his work when he sadden
ly drops everything and goes away.
What is needed on the isthmuslis or
ganization of the laborers. They
should be divided into gangs with a
skilled man at the head of eaah gang
to Instruct them In the work and to
advance those who become proficient
in any line. Organization along this
line is under way at the present time."
Poor Opinion of Chinese Labor.
Governor Magoon is not favorably
Impressed with the Chinese as a fac
tor in the construction of the canal.
"There are at present from 2,000 to
3,000 Chinese on the Isthmus," said
Governor Magoon, "and as a citizen I
do not think they mike desirable la
borers. They cannot be depended
upon as much as can the Barbados or
native negro.
"As soon as a Chinaman earns
enough money he starts a shop of his
own and the government loses a man.
The good results that have been at
tained in several parts of the world by
the use of coolie labor have been due
to great power exertod by the coolie
contractors. This would almost be a
system of peonage that the United
States would not tolerate. If we were
to employ coolie labor we would have
to depend on the Chinaman as an in
dividual, and, in my opinion, that
would not do, as the coolie will work
as little as possible unless under con
tract. I feel certain that the Chinese
would not be a benefit to the United
8tates .....
PRESIDENT PALMA' PENNILESS.
8pent His Entire Fortune In the Cause
of His Country.
Washington, Oct. 2.—Senor Qoe
sada, the Cuban minister, has not re
ceived any reply from Secretary Taft
to his lotter of resignation transmitted
Saturday. The retiring minister en
tertains toward former President
Palma feelings of the greatest attach
ment and sympathy.
"After spending forty of the seventy
three years of his life in fighting for
Cuban liberty President Palma leaves
the palace in Havana without a dol
lar," says Senor Quesada. "His pri
vate fortune, as well as the best ener
gies of his life, have been patriotically
poured out in the cause of his country.
"I have cabled to President Palma
an invitation to make my house his
home if he decides to come to the
United States. Although President
Palma is practically penniless he has
hosts of friends who will remain stead
fast even though adversity has be
come his portion."
Mr. Quesada made known for the
first time that a year ago he had ten
dered his resignation, but that Pres
ident Palma had declined to accept it.
He said It was not his purpose to re
turn to Cuba and participate in the
election to be arranged by the pro
visional government, as he had no
ambitions for future office.
'Pension List Decreases.
Washington, Oct. 2.—The net de
erease in the pension roll of th«
United Staies for the fiscal year end
Ing Juno 30, last, amounted to 12,470
the largest decrease ever known in
the history of the country.

San Francisco, Oct. 2.—The Mar
chants' Exchange has received a cable
gram from Honolulu saying that tin
United States army transport Sh?r
ldan has been floated and la proceed

WITH ALL POSSIBLE SPEED.
Preparations for Shipment of Troops
to Cuba Proceeding.
Washington, Oct. 2.—Secretary Taft
la in constant communication with
President Roosevelt concerning the
Cuban situation, but has aot seen fit
to communicate with the war depart
ment officials since Saturday. Prep
arations for the first expedition to
Cuba are being hastened with all pos
sible speed. Officers and clerks at
the war department worked practically
all night and advices from tho troops
ordered to mobilize at Newport News
indicate that they will be ready as
soon a 3 transports are available.
The Second battalion of engineers
left here during the day to go aboard
the Sumner at New York.
Two complete field hospital outfits
will go to Cuba, sailing on the first
transport leaving Newport News.
Another company of the hospital
corps is on Its way from San Francisco
to Newport News. Each of the seven
regiments rtrdered to Cuba will have
a regimental hospital and a full qi^pta
of medical ofllcors.
HARRIMAN TO GET CONTROL.
Deal Involving the Wisconsin Central
Railroad.
Milwaukee, Oct. 2.—Tfce Journal
•ays:
Again it is said that a deal Involv
ing the control of the Wisconsin Cen
tral railroad is pending, if it has not
practically been consummated.
General Counsel Howard Morris Is
said to have been in New York during
the past month for no other reason
than to work out the details of turn
ing over the Wisconsin Central to Ed
ward H. Ilarriman.
No one in a position to talk of the
rejorted deal at the offices of tho
Wisconsin Central could b^ jeen. Gen
eral Counsel Morris declared that ho
was too busy to talk on the subject.
New York Democratic Chairman.
New York, Oct. 2.—The Democratic
state committee met at the Hoffman
tlouse and elected William J. Connors
of Buffalo chairman without opposi
tion. Mr. Conners led the fight at
Buffalo for the nomination of Mr.
Hearst. After the meeting most of
the members of the state committee
were received by Mr. Haarat at his
repldenco.

Listen,the Thor even got
the corporal's socks cle
The corporal had on a pair
of new-issue shoes that fitted him "too
soon" in spots. We were slogging through
some pretty muggy stuff and he
took his shoes off. We bet he’d
never get his socks clean, but the
laugh was on us after he had
used the Thor Washer we’ve got
in camp.
Imagine having a
Thor away out here, just like the
one at home. Only here it works every
day and all day, with guys standing in
line. Every day is Monday here for the
Thor. It sure takes a beating, like wash
ing the corporal’s socks,
'VL but nothing fazes it.
The only Thors be
ing made now are being
• built for the Army and
Navy. The Thor you might have owned is
probably in some far off camp or outpost
just where you’d want it to be.
Day after day these ‘Thors are
fighting dirt for men who fight—in steaming
jungles or frozen uplands of the north.
In the meantime, our engineers
have completed plans for an Automatic Thor
that does things you never dreamed were pos
sible—and does them automatically. Wait till
you see it!
Buy bonds and more bonds
now, to hurry the day of peace, and to be sure
of a new Thor right after the war. One will be
ready for you then . . . and it will be the pride
and joy of your household.

| Civil Courts May Get
Dog Ownership Case
I Ownership of Sporty, a little black
mongrel dog, was still unsettled
after Municipal Court Judge Ar
mond W. Scott yesterday dismissed
charges of larceny of the dog
against Mrs. Eines Byrd, colored, of
l the 900 block of M street N.W.
Mrs. Louise Bennett, colored, of
the 1100 block of Tenth street N.W.,
told John Lane, assistant United
States attorney, she intended to
take the matter to the courts, even
though offers of other pets have
been flowing in from sympathetic
dog lovers.
Mrs. Bennett’s son. Miller, 11, tes
tified at a trial last March 22 that
Mrs. Byrd tool: the dog away from
him in November as he led Sporty
on a leash at Ninth and M streets
N.W. He said the dog had been his
since he was six weeks old, nearly a
year. Mrs. Byrd declared Sporty is
the dog which was given to her in
1943 and disappeared in November,
1944.
Municipal Court Judge Thomas
D. Quinn at that time declared a
mistrial when a material defense
witness, alleged to have given the
dog to Mrs. Byrd, failed to appear
in court.
The case yesterday was dismissed
at the request of Mr. Lane who said
the evidence in the first trial showed
Mrs. Byrd claimed the dog as hers
in good faith and this was sufficient
to defeat a charge of larceny. Mrs.'
Bennett still has recourse in the
civil courts, he said.
WILLIAM ROSENDORF

ICancer of Skin Declared 95% Curable in First Stages I
| Cancer of the skin is 95 per cent
!j curable when diagnosed and treat
ed properly in its early stages, ac
cording to a new pamphlet just
published by the Cancer Institute
of the National Instiute of Health.
The pamphlet, entitled “Cancer
of the Skin,” was prepared as part
of a co-operative effort with the
American Cancer Society to pro
mote public knowledge of how best
to combat cancer.
Most skin cancer appears in
elderly persons, the publication
points out, and occurs most fre
quently in geographical areas
which have long periods of sun
light. It occurs in persons with
light or ruddy complexions more
frequently than in those with dark
skins.
The pamphlet points out that
any skin sore, burn, scar, or in
flammatory lesion that does not
heal promptly should be brought
to a doctor’s attention, although
very few of these may be can
cerous.
Pastes, ointments or advertised
cures arc especially dangerous in
the treatment of skin cancer, the
pamphlet says, since any self-1
treatment Is apt to alter the ap
pearance of the skin and make
diagnosis difficult.
The publication is one of a
series on different types of can
cer prepared by the institute in
co-operation with the cancer so
ciety.
U. of Va. Calls Parley

ty the Associated Press
CHARLOTTESVILLE, Va., June
8.—Problems of Federal taxation
will be discussed at a three-day
conference for lawyers, account
ants and others at the University
of Virginia, beginning Thursday.
Randolph E. Paul, author and
lecturer on tax problems and for
merly a tax adviser to the Secre
tary of the Treasury, will address
a dinner meeting on June 15.
